title: Jokes and the Logic of the Cognitive Unconscious
venue: Massachusetts Institute of Technology Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, A.I. Memo No. 603
research_questions: How can humorous nonsense be explained in a way that makes it continuous with Freud’s account of jokes about forbidden subjects such as aggression and sexuality?
What role do knowledge about knowledge, common-sense reasoning failures, and unconscious censor-like mechanisms play in humor?
Why do jokes become less funny when repeated, and what does this imply about learning and mental censorship?
How do analogy, metaphor, frame-shifting, and faulty reasoning contribute to humor?

methods_proposed.name: Cognitive censor theory of humor
methods_proposed.summary: The essay proposes that humor helps minds learn to recognize and suppress 'bugs'—ineffective or destructive thought processes—through censor-like mechanisms analogous to Freud’s emotional censors but applied to reasoning and cognition.

methods_proposed.name: Frame-substitution account of humor
methods_proposed.summary: The essay argues that many forms of humor involve unexpected frame-substitution, where a situation is first represented from one viewpoint and then suddenly reinterpreted through another frame, often revealing a faulty or prohibited inference.

methods_proposed.name: Two-brain A/B censor model
methods_proposed.summary: The essay sketches a model in which an A-brain interacts with the world while a B-brain monitors internal A-states and can suppress undesirable processes such as meandering, circling, or confusion before they fully develop.

humor_theories.detail: Freud’s theory is described as explaining jokes through unconscious censors, forbidden thoughts, release of psychic energy, and laughter, but the essay argues that this account works less well for humorous nonsense.
The essay explains humor through frames, defaults, analogies, metaphor, and 'unexpected frame-substitution,' in which a scene is suddenly viewed in a different way.

The paper’s own account treats humor as related to learning and communicating about bugs in common-sense reasoning, with laughter disrupting faulty reasoning while focusing attention for censor learning.

key_findings: The essay argues that common-sense logic is too unreliable for practical use without learned mechanisms that prevent recurring reasoning failures.
Minsky proposes that the mind builds 'cognitive censors' that suppress unproductive mental states, analogous to Freud’s emotional censors but directed at faulty reasoning and nonsense.
Humor is presented as a mechanism for learning and communicating about cognitive bugs, especially failures involving paradox, analogy, metaphor, and frame-shifting.
The essay claims that jokes become less funny when repeated because censor learning has already occurred for that joke or joke type.
Laughter is theorized to both disrupt further reasoning along a prohibited or ridiculous path and focus attention on the absurdity so censor-learning can absorb it.

The paper argues that humor lacks sharp boundaries because it evolved from overlapping mechanisms involving laughter, faulty reasoning, taboos, prohibitions, conciliation, aggression, sexuality, and unconscious suppression.
limitations: The paper is a theoretical essay and reports no experiments, datasets, computational implementation, or quantitative evaluation.
Minsky explicitly says he makes no plea for his particular evolutionary scenario, only that something of the general sort must have happened.
The essay argues that it is probably futile to ask precisely what humor is, because 'humor' points to different web-models in different minds rather than a sharply bounded thing.
The paper notes that adult humor complicates the theory because later cultural and individual development can exploit and rearrange earlier mechanisms.
The author states that psychological theories can usually describe only fragments of a larger web of interacting subsystems rather than completely explain a surface behavior.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper contains a note on ethnic humor, suggesting that it may involve aggression and also a representational tendency to project stupidity jokes onto alien stereotypes; it warns that this can become a runaway process and that prejudice will be hard to eradicate without understanding the value and role of stereotypes in ordinary thinking.
